body_text: You are a structured-output adapter for Cognee. Return ONLY one valid JSON object. No markdown fences, no commentary. frontmatter.title: You are a structured-output adapter for Cognee.
frontmatter.summary: You are a structured-output adapter for Cognee. Return ONLY one valid JSON object. No markdown fences, no commentary. The JSON MUST validate against this Pydantic JSON Schema for KnowledgeGraph: { "$defs": { "Edge": { "description": "Edge in a knowledge graph.", "properties": { "source_node_id": { "title": "Source Node Id", "type": "string" }, "target_node_id": { "title": "Target Node Id", "type": "string" }, "relationship_name": { "title": "Relationship Name", "type": "string" }, "properties": { "anyOf": [ { "type": "object" }, { "type": "null" } ], "default": null, "description": "A dictionary of properties associated with the edge.", "title": "Properties" } }, "required": [ "source_node_id", "target_node_id", "relationship_name" ], "title": "Edge", "type": "object" }, "Node": { "description": "Node in a knowledge graph.", "properties": { "id": { "title": "Id", "type": "string" }, "name": { "title": "Name", "type": "string" }, "type": { "title": "Type", "type": "string" }, "description": { "title": "Description", "type": "string" }, "properties": { "anyOf": [ { "type": "object" }, { "type": "null" } ], "default": null, "description": "A dictionary of properties associated with th
commands_run:
python3 - <<'PY'
import json, collections, re
from pathlib import Path
p=Path('output/cognee_codex_full_v2_bundle/edges.jsonl')
rel=collections.Counter()
prefix=collections.Counter()
target_prefix=collections.Counter()
examples=[]
with p.open() as f:
    for i,line in enumerate(f,1):
        obj=json.loads(line)
        rel[obj['relationship']]+=1
        prefix[obj['source_id'].split(':',1)[0]]+=1
        target_prefix[obj['target_id'].split(':',1)[0]]+=1
        if i<=5: examples.append(obj)
print('relationships', rel.most_common(20))
print('source prefixes', prefix.most_common(20))
print('target prefixes', target_prefix.most_common(20))
PY

python3 -c "import json,collections,pathlib; p=pathlib.Path('output/cognee_codex_full_v2_bundle/edges.jsonl');
counts=collections.Counter();
for line in p.open():
 o=json.loads(line)
 key=(o['relationship'], o['source_id'].split(':',1)[0], o['target_id'])
 counts[key]+=1
for key in [
 ('uses','Paper','MethodologicalConcept:gaussian-splatting:4f4b246c6522'),
 ('belongs_to_approach_family','Paper','ApproachFamily:gaussian-splatting-reconstruction:84097d3185ea'),
 ('addresses','Paper','ResearchTopic:world-model:df0bb7d75cb8'),
 ('addresses','Paper','ResearchTopic:visual-slam:5e1e69bfac67'),
 ('part_of','Paper','ResearchField:3d-4d-vision-and-reconstruction:4273d84d58f1'),
 ('part_of','Paper','ResearchField:research-literature:8be73da23ed0'),
 ('part_of','Paper','ResearchField:llm-reasoning:530f2afb39c4')
]:
 print(key, counts[key])"

turns.text: I’m reading `output/cognee_codex_full_v2_bundle/edges.jsonl` to extract the entities and relationships into the required knowledge-graph schema.
The file is a large JSONL edge dump. I’m sampling its structure and counting edge and node patterns so the output graph stays simple and faithful to the data.

turns.name: exec_command
turns.text:
{"cmd":"python3 - <<'PY'
import json, collections, re
from pathlib import Path
p=Path('output/cognee_codex_full_v2_bundle/edges.jsonl')
rel=collections.Counter()
prefix=collections.Counter()
target_prefix=collections.Counter()
examples=[]
with p.open() as f:
    for i,line in enumerate(f,1):
        obj=json.loads(line)
        rel[obj['relationship']]+=1
        prefix[obj['source_id'].split(':',1)[0]]+=1
        target_prefix[obj['target_id'].split(':',1)[0]]+=1
        if i<=5: examples.append(obj)
print('relationships', rel.most_common(20))
print('source prefixes', prefix.most_common(20))
print('target prefixes', target_prefix.most_common(20))
PY","workdir":"/Users/neo/Developer/Projects/LLM-Wiki","max_output_tokens":4000,"yield_time_ms":1000}
The sandbox blocks temporary heredoc files, so I’m switching to a single-command parser to inspect the JSONL without writing anything.

turns.name: exec_command
exec_command

turns.text: I have enough structure now. I’m checking a few dominant counts so the final graph can use concrete properties instead of vague summaries.
